item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably, the closest match being an Amazon ad from 2008. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It looks like some of the D-class numbers in the test log got mixed up.
Good SCP otherwise, though. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending was especially great. You get an upvote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole time, the one thing I felt was missing was a denial of the predictions coming true if the instructions weren't followed. And then you added in that last addendum. :D Which, I agree with the analysis above, it doesn't actually confirm or deny anything, and that's why it's perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: was about to downvote until the last collapsible. That was cute. Main issue with it though is: How is "most trustworthy voice" determined? I don't even know who I would consider most trustworthy to me. Carl Sagan? Neil DeGrasse Tyson? Walter Cronkite? An acquaintance with no sense of etiquette and never bothers with polite "white lies"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote worthy, good job! The only thing I can think of is maybe delving deeper into the experiment logs, though that may completely alter the flow of the addenda.

Also, creepy potential breach scenario if the SCP were slightly different:
1283: "Do you remember X?
SCP-1283 will breach containment/'the signal will become global'
You must eliminate the staff of Site Y"
Foundation: "What do, what do?!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I know this is sorta performing necromancy at this point, but seeing as how no one responded to your changes, I just thought I'd give feedback, even if it's years after the fact.

I thought the addition of a minor containment breach years after the warning was excellent. It was my reason for upvoting for a few reasons.

1) The event was so far after the warning, that it still leaves it ambiguous whether SCP-1283 really knows anything or not.

2) The breach, while involving several SCPs, seems mostly innocuous as breaches go. There were casualties, but the punishment being only a formal reprimand makes me think that it wasn't that big a deal.

3) If SCP-1283 DID know about this breach, and that it was a relatively minor one that did not result in a huge catastrophe, then the method for resolving it (killing someone) and the panic state instilled in the subject was pretty disproportionate to the end result (the D-class would have been routinely executed by the time the breach took place years later, so there was no need for him to panic over it).

3) If the method for stopping the event from that last test was disproportionate to the actual harm, then what about the other tests? "The authors grow impatient" could literally be something as mundane as, say, several authors impatiently waiting on an editor to get back to them. Maybe all kidnapping a child would do is put something on TV they could all follow without getting bored. Completely unnecessary and disproportionate to the amount of harm letting a few authors become impatient would cause.

Upvoted for creepy implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In light of the feedback I've received, I'm considering one of two revisions.

1) I make a simple note at the end saying something like "SCP-1283's use of the word "breach" has been noted, the facility has been placed at a temporary heightened alert status" and leave it at that.

or

2) I add an incident report dated months, maybe years later mentioning Dr. Ivan ██████ being responsible for a minor containment breach due to some innocuous mistake. Explicit enough to be relevant, but taking place a long time after the actual statement that there's no way of truly knowing whether SCP-1283 actually predicted it.

Opinions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. I think it has enough foreboding and random without going over the top. (I like well-written random. Which I think this is. I can see the secret-spy-phrases inspiration behind it, and I like that secret message that only means something to one person thing.) And it leaves me wanting to know more. I want to read more tests! Except don't put more in because it if it revealed everything and actually did have more tests, I think that would be a bad thing. Leaving me going, "And then what happened…? What happened in the next test…!" Is a mark of all my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, you've caught me. Yeah, that's a picture of a modern model, but I wasn't able to find a nice, clear picture of an actual 1930's radio of that style. As for the AM/FM switch being explicitly mentioned, I felt like it would only call more attention to it if I pretended it wasn't there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It illustrates that the radio is responding to the real world, it knew Ivan was there and all. That's an important point to establish, and you managed it well.

But I think one of the Foundation's primary research goals would be determing if these were valid predictions or not. Seeing the future is a Big Deal after all. I just don't see any competent scientist failing to note any correlating events or lack thereof.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The presence of an "AM/FM switch" is probably an anachronism, as the first experimental FM radio station was only licensed for construction in 1937, and there were only a half million FM-capable units manufactured by 1945. The image attached to the article falls victim to this as well, because it shows a modern reproduction in the 1930s style suitable for listening to today's FM frequency band.

I liked the SCP, though. Especially the incident log - one would think that alarm bells would be going off in Doc Ivan's head after hearing about a "breach", even though the message wasn't directed to him, since that word has special connotations at the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this, my only gripe is making the voice and the conversation sub-SCPs. That doesn't seem to work. It's a radio, and the voice comes out of it, so that fits with it, it doesn't need an additional number, and the conversation is a process. Look at, I don't know, 173. It leaves blood and feces on the floor through an unknown process, but that substrate doesn't become SCP-173-1. Still upvoting though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool first SCP. Quite scary, and I like how it doesn't really go over the top, and the event log and incident are interesting to read. i don't know why, but from the age, look and abilities of the thing I got the vibe of one such warning, occuring sometimes in past, about Adolf Hitler's rise. bravo, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel that in this particular article, the "lolrandom" bits. says is part of the horror actually, at least it is for me. At the beginning of the addenda, a reader would be like "okay, the radio is just doing silly stuff". As it progresses, the reader sees that the radio is potentially dangerous. And by the end, the reader realizes that the radio is knowledgeable of the name of the doctor that is in that very room.

One that was creepy, but two, it made me think back and ask, wait…were all of those other commands legitimate? What did the key do? Did the music stop? And if it did, what happened? Was there a breach? Why wasn't this investigated? Is it the radio aware of the future? What force is behind this and what does it want to accomplish?

edit: It also just occurred to me that I think that I may have justsimply said what General Harland had already said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I also considered those tests, but I didn't include them for two reasons, one in-universe and one just my own decision.

One, I felt it was already running too long. Adding even more test logs, that are even more specific, wouldn't help that.

Two, they were planning to, but then Incident-239-A occurred, and testing was discontinued. They might pick it up again later, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would think the Foundation would do a few more tests:

1) Ask about the "bad thing". "Which well is going to run dry?" "Why is the well running dry a bad thing?" etc.

2) See what would happen if the subject is prevented from hearing the third phrase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job on your first article — I really liked this with the exception of the interview log. It actually worked better with just the test logs, IMHO. Still, an enjoyable read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, flow was a big problem for me when constructing this. As for the SCP-1283-2 designation, that was me feeling that it read awkwardly to constantly have it as "all of the information stated by SCP-1283-1." Additionally, I felt that I had put in as much absurdity as I could without it detracting from the article. I wanted to avoid "you need to hop on one foot" or similarly silly instructions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered doing so, but I felt that answering whether a containment breach took place, either way, was a little bit too… blatant. I had hoped that referencing having it directly reference Ivan ██████ and "this room" would be enough to provide that clue to the readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, sorry that you feel that way. Do you have any suggestions for improvement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's a good effort, just on a really lackluster and repeatedly-done-before idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But was there a breach?

The rest of the predictions are so delphic (lookee! I invented an adjective!) that researchers would never be able to tell if they happened or not. But a breach is a specific enough reference that I think they would note whether or not any happened in a reasonable correlation. Whether there was one or not wouldn't neccesarily conclusively prove or disprove the predictions, but it could clue the readers in on if the radio knew what it was talkign about or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The structure of this one really is odd. There's no reason for the content of the message to be broken out with its own designation, especially given how badly it affects the flow of the article. The testing logs are good, but that final addendum adds nothing. If your only goal is to show greater awareness from the object, have its instructions be absurd- perhaps to remove the doctor's hat, else something more elaborate. Absurdity and the inexplicably odd are your friends in an article like this. No vote for now, but you're in the right ballpark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Raven Mackenzie for adding appropriate tags, it had completely slipped my mind to do so myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't exactly shooting for "trying to escape," just more of a "oh it has more awareness of things than we thought."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, and yes, that was definitely something I was striving to avoid.

"Oh and if they don't do X their faces explode" always just feels like a lazy method of tying things up at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What? Why? What do those things have to do with each other?

Honestly, that's the kind of reaction I was hoping to get from that set up. It wasn't an attempt to be lolrandom, I wanted to give it a sense of "Well, this clearly does something, and at least one person seems to get it, but so far we've got nothing."

I was partially inspired by the old spy novel trope of "trigger phrases." Innocuous phrases, meaningless to anyone except the person it's intended for, that trigger specific behaviors in the target. However, I did my best to avoid cheesy "THE FOX IS AMONG THE HENS" spy speak, but unfortunately a little bit of clarity may have been lost in that endeavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I didn't care much for this radio. However, after reading it a bit and looking at it with a different perspective I find it growing on me. In a weird sort of way, it feels like a send-off of ritual creepypastas, the only thing missing would be if the subjects ears exploded if they didn't do what the radio wanted (note that I am not advocating this and it would make me downvote if you did that).

All in all, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as LolFoundation was something that was once kind of accepted and now isn't, I'm starting to feel that RandumFoundation should start to go the same way. Sometimes when there's nonsensical and whimsical bullshit it can work (some of which I am guilty of upvoting), but I feel that now too many conceptually dull SCPs are getting positive reviews just because of randomness in the addendum sections.

Phase 1: "Do you remember your first car?"
Phase 2: "Something bad is going to happen. The well is going to run dry."
Phase 3: "You can stop it. You need to break a plate."

What? Why? What do those things have to do with each other? Why does this machine ask this of people? Why does it exist? There's no reason given, unless I'm severely overlooking something. And honestly, it doesn't hold up on its own. There's no connection to be made.

There's nothing wrong with giving your SCP a little personality, but if that's what you have to rely on to make it likeable, then you should probably revise your idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, as Vincent Redgrave mentioned below, the Etruscan telemarketer. Also, SCP-1954.

Still, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple sentence like "Researchers have yet to find any correlation between predictions made by SCP-1283 and actual events." should work. The Foundation isn't ruling out the possibility, but the audience reading it will get the hint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the steady and vaguely ritualistic nature of this. The last log seems a bit iffy, though. I don't feel like this object should be trying to escape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of that sheep liver ritual thing with the telemarketer voice from a week or two ago, but I think it's different enough to stand on its own. Well done for a first article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, I hear ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately I couldn't think of a way to make that clearer without making it sound like "The Foundation monitored literally everything and found that nothing changed anywhere."

If you have suggestions, I welcome them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh yeah?
Well I value your feedback!

Poor tone.

Fixed. Now reads "very little."

Poor tone.

Fixed. Removed.

Should be a question mark here.

Unless I'm mistaken, no there shouldn't, because the sentence does not end there. Feel free to correct me.

Poor tone.

Those are descriptions of the nature of the warnings. If you know more clinical synonyms, I welcome them.

Poor tone.

Fixed. Removed "incredibly."

I don't know if "above average reason" is a thing.

Altered to read "reasoning abilities."

Again, don't know if that's a thing.

It is. Situations that cause panic. Containment breaches, D-Class riots, what have you.

Unnecessary comma.

Removed.

I would recommend de-capitalizing "Subject" and placing "the" before it. It reads weird.

I feel it reads oddly as "the subject." Opinions, etc.

Can I ask what this reminds you of? If it's been done before I want to ensure I've differentiated myself enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is alright. No vote for now, criticism below.

+ Criticism
-

remarkably little

Poor tone.

long-since

Poor tone.

Do you remember

Should be a question mark here.

cryptic, meaningless

Poor tone.

incredibly dangerous,

Poor tone.

above average calm and reason

I don't know if "above average reason" is a thing.

panic situations

Again, don't know if that's a thing.

inconsolable, and displays

Unnecessary comma.

Test logs in general

I would recommend de-capitalizing "Subject" and placing "the" before it. It reads weird.

Overall, I feel like this has been done before, but this is still original and interesting enough to pull it off. However, the tone errors hold it back. I'll upvote when those are fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well you got it, and it was subtle too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to be safe, don't read it again later. Wouldn't want to spoil it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an inexplicable liking for this. It might be because I just woke up though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slightly funny is what I was going for

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the overall concept, mind-altering things always creep me out. Also,

Testing has observed SCP-1283-1 belonging to….. on one occasion, a news anchor from the ██████ Network….. The voice chosen by SCP-1283-1 belongs to whoever the subject considers to be the most trustworthy

Am I the only one who finds this slightly funny?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok… I already doubt that the voice should even be considered a separate entity (though I can see it working like that), so the conversation being separate from the voice just seems silly to me. Just my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1283 is the radio itself, -01 is the voice, -02 is the conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused. Is SCP-1283-01 the radio and -02 the voice, or is -01 the voice and -02 the conversation itself?

Otherwise, I like the idea. Maybe a bit more work is needed to fix any confusion or tone issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Flow was something I struggled with throughout this SCP's creation and revisions. I regret not being able to make it work a bit better, but nothing I tried was able to balance flow with clarity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A couple grammar and tone issues, but otherwise, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A sort of interesting concept with some lackluster execution. The way you listed the objects effect killed the flow, and it felt very awkward overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't really dig it until the log, then I upvoted. I think I would like it more of it was explicit that this in no way is telling the future, that the predictions are nonsensical and unrelated to reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my first attempt at an SCP.

Give me your best shot, I can take it, I'll only cry a little bit.
